coredata.dc:title: Green and active hydrogen production from hydrolysis of ammonia borane by using caffeine carbon quantum dot-supported ruthenium catalyst in methanol solvent by hydrothermal treatment
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2023 Hydrogen Energy Publications LLC. Published by Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: Hydrogen Energy Publications LLC. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: İzgi̇, Mehmet Sait
Onat, Erhan
Şahi̇n, Ömer
Saka, Cafer
coredata.dc:description: Here, carbon quantum dot (CQD) supported Ru nanoparticles from the caffeine in methanol or water by the hydrothermal method were synthesized for the first time to produce hydrogen (H2) by hydrolysis of ammonia borane(NH3BH3) and their catalytic activities were investigated. The chemical compositions and morphology structures of this caffeine carbon dot-supported Ru catalysts were carefully characterized by UV, PL, TEM, FTIR, and ICP/OES analyses. Well-dispersed Ru nanoclusters with ∼1.81 nm particle size on CQD by the hydrothermal method in methanol medium showed very good catalytic performance. The turnover frequency (TOF) obtained for H2 production from the catalytic hydrolysis of 5% NH3BH3 with CQD supported Ru catalyst obtained by the hydrothermal method in methanol and water was found to be 361 and 1895 mol H2 min−1 mol−1, respectively. The activation energies (Ea) for NH3BH3 hydrolysis with the same catalysts were measured as 33.32 and 27.80 kJmol-1, respectively.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Caffeine carbon quantum dot
Ruthenium
Methanol
Hydrothermal treatment
Hydrogen

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Carbon quantum dot and catalyst synthesis

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: UV–visible absorption spectra (a) and fluorescence spectra (b) of CQD (caffeine) and Ru@CQD
TEM analyses and average particle sizes CQD (a, b), Ru@CQD (water) (c, d) and Ru@CQD (methanol) (e, f) nanocomposites.
FTIR spectra of Ru@CQD (water) and Ru@CQD (methanol) nanocomposites.
Effect of metal mole ratio of caffeine-supported Ru catalyst obtained by hydrothermal treatment in water medium and without hydrothermal treatment on NH3BH3 hydrolysis.
Effect of caffeine-supported Ru catalyst obtained by hydrothermal treatment in different solvents (water, methanol and ethanol) on NH3BH3 hydrolysis.
Effect of NH3BH3 concentration on the hydrolysis using caffeine-supported Ru catalyst obtained by hydrothermal treatment in water (a) and methanol (b).
Effect of NaOH concentration on the NH3BH3 hydrolysis using caffeine-supported Ru catalyst obtained by hydrothermal treatment in water (a) and methanol (b).
Effect of temperature on the NH3BH3 hydrolysis using caffeine-supported Ru catalyst obtained by hydrothermal treatment in water (a) and methanol (b).
Arrhenius plots (ln k vs 1/T) obtained with Ru@CQD (water) (a) and Ru@CQD (methanol) (b) at different temperatures.
H2 volumes obtained from six consecutive repeatability experiments of Ru@CQD (water) (a) and Ru@CQD (methanol) (b) catalysts to H2 production from NH3BH3 hydrolysis.
The production scheme of Ru@CQD (methanol or water) catalyst and H2 production from NH3BH3 hydrolysis.
HGR and TOF values obtained by the effect of NH3BH3 concentration on the caffeine-supported Ru catalyst obtained by hydrothermal treatment in water and methanol solvents.
The comparison of the obtained TOF and Ea values with different catalyst systems used for NH3BH3 in the literature.

head.ce:title: Green and active hydrogen production from hydrolysis of ammonia borane by using caffeine carbon quantum dot-supported ruthenium catalyst in methanol solvent by hydrothermal treatment

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Faculty of Engineering and Architecture, Department of Chemical Engineering, Siirt University, 56100 Siirt, Turkey
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Faculty of Engineering and Architecture, Department of Chemical Engineering, Siirt University, 56100 Siirt, Turkey

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Tatvan Vocational School, Bitlis Eren University, Bitlis, Turkey
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Tatvan Vocational School, Bitlis Eren University, Bitlis, Turkey

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemical Engineering, Istanbul Technical University, Istanbul, Turkey
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemical Engineering, Istanbul Technical University, Istanbul, Turkey

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Faculty of Health Science, Siirt University, 56100 Siirt, Turkey
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Faculty of Health Science, Siirt University, 56100 Siirt, Turkey

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Here, carbon quantum dot (CQD) supported Ru nanoparticles from the caffeine in methanol or water by the hydrothermal method were synthesized for the first time to produce hydrogen (H2) by hydrolysis of ammonia borane(NH3BH3) and their catalytic activities were investigated. The chemical compositions and morphology structures of this caffeine carbon dot-supported Ru catalysts were carefully characterized by UV, PL, TEM, FTIR, and ICP/OES analyses. Well-dispersed Ru nanoclusters with ∼1.81 nm particle size on CQD by the hydrothermal method in methanol medium showed very good catalytic performance. The turnover frequency (TOF) obtained for H2 production from the catalytic hydrolysis of 5% NH3BH3 with CQD supported Ru catalyst obtained by the hydrothermal method in methanol and water was found to be 361 and 1895 mol H2 min−1 mol−1, respectively. The activation energies (Ea) for NH3BH3 hydrolysis with the same catalysts were measured as 33.32 and 27.80 kJmol-1, respectively.

ce:section.ce:para:
Due to its high efficiency and limited environmental impact, hydrogen is considered a clean energy carrier [1]. It is important to obtain and develop suitable hydrogen storage materials such as high hydrogen storage capacity and controllable release rate [2]. Ammonia borane (NH3BH3), which is among the chemical hydrides, has an important place in the field of chemical hydrogen storage due to its advantages such as high hydrogen content of 19.6% by weight, easy transport and chemical stability [3]. The production of H2 by hydrolysis of NH3BH3 under suitable conditions (Eq. (1)) is also given.
(1) NH3BH3 + 2H2O → NH4 + + BO2 − + 3H2 (g)

However, a suitable catalyst is required for this process to occur as desired. A wide variety of homogeneous and heterogeneous catalysts have been developed so far for researchers [4–9]. However, nanocatalysts obtained by the coordination of mono- or multi-metals leading to higher catalytic activity tend to form larger aggregates. Thus, this reduces the number of activity sites required for the catalytic reaction to proceed. Moreover, for these nanocatalysts to show proper activity, good dispersibility in the reaction medium is generally important to increase the contact probability between metal nanoparticles and reactants. Support materials are a very important factor for the good dispersibility of these nanocatalysts in the reaction medium [10–12]. In addition, the support materials must contain significant active sites for strong metal-support interaction. At the same time, the small size (<10 nm) of the catalysts in the hydrolysis of NH3BH3 causes an increase in the surface area and hence the number of active sites, thus affecting the catalytic performance [13]. Carbon materials are widely used for metal catalysts because of their chemical stability, low price, high metal loading capacity and excellent conductivity for support materials [14]. In recent years, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) among nanostructured carbon materials have come into focus for researchers due to their chemical and physical properties, such as particle size usually below 10 nm and the surface containing polar organic groups such as carboxylate, hydroxyl and/or amino groups [15,16].

There are different approaches to CQD production [17–19]. The production of CQDs by the hydrothermal method has important features such as low cost, environmentally friendly and non-toxic production. The basic logic in hydrothermal production is the breakdown of organic structure into molecules at high temperatures and pressure [20,21]. In the production of CQDs, a high-temperature hydrothermal method is used that allows the interaction between precursors and water molecules [22,23]. The hydrothermal method, which produces high amounts of quantum dots, may be considered a better way to fabricate CQDs. Better surface passivation and photoluminescent properties of CQDs can be achieved with high production yields [24]. The hydrothermal method involves dehydration, polymerization, carbonization and passivation of raw materials in the process of CQD synthesis. At the same time, the abundance of oxygenated functional feedstock groups contributes to the formation of carbon nuclei and surface functionalization of CQDs [25].

In most of the hydrothermal production methods of CQDs from raw materials, deionized water is chosen as the reaction medium because the carbon sources are water-soluble and well-dispersible. However, if there are oxygen-functional groups in the chemical structures of the raw materials, other options can also be evaluated [22]. It is important in terms of production cost that CQDs prepared from different carbon sources are green and cheap sources. Caffeine, an active alkaloid component found in many natural products, is consumed in coffee, tea and beverages. Pure caffeine is a white odourless powder [26]. The size and morphological properties of the catalyst can be affected by the solvent used. Catalytic performances of catalysts with different physical and chemical properties can also be affected [27]. Catalysts prepared in different solvent environments for use in NH3BH3 hydrolysis have not been encountered and a limited number of studies have been carried out on this subject. In this study, caffeine CQD-supported Ru catalysts were synthesized in methanol, water and ethanol solvents with different polarities and viscosities.

To our knowledge, there are no reports of H2 production from NH3BH3 hydrolysis with caffeine-based CQD-supported Ru catalysts. Here, CQDs were synthesized from caffeine by a hydrothermal process in different solvent environments. This catalyst was used for the first time to obtain H2 from NH3BH3 hydrolysis with Ru catalyst as support. The properties of the samples obtained by ultraviolet, fluorescent, TEM, FTIR and ICP-OES analyses were investigated.

All chemicals were used without further purification. NH3BH3 (Sigma-Aldrich, 97%), RuCl3.6H2O (Energy Chemical Co., Ltd., 99%), NaOH (Sigma-Aldrich), caffeine (Sigma-Aldrich), ethanol (Tekkim, Turkey) and methanol (Tekkim, Turkey) was used.
Caffeine (C8H10N4O2), known as 1,3,7-trimethylxanthine, is often used as a stimulant in beverages such as coffee, tea, and soft drinks [28].
The surface morphologies of the catalysts by TEM (Hitachi High Tech HT7700) were analysed.

The surfactant groups of the catalysts were determined by the FTIR spectrum (Nicolet 520). The Ru content in the supported catalyst composite was analysed by inductively coupled plasma (ICP, Agilent 7800 model). Absorption spectra of the carbon spot sample were obtained with a UV–Vis/fluorescence spectrophotometer (PerkinElmer LS 55 Fluorescence).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Carbon quantum dot and catalyst synthesis
ce:section.ce:para: To produce CQD from caffeine by hydrothermal method, a certain amount of caffeine and purified water was first left in the furnace. The hydrothermal temperature was studied at 120, 150, 165 and 180 °C. Hydrothermal waiting times of 2, 4, 8 and 16 h were chosen. The best hydrothermal production conditions were tried to be determined at different temperatures and residence times according to the photoluminescence effect under UV light (360 nm). As the optimum parameters for the hydrothermal treatment, the waiting time was 8 h and the temperature was 180 °C. Upon determination of the best light absorption under UV light, CQD synthesis was performed and its adsorption was monitored in a UV-Fluorescent Spectrophotometer. The produced CQD was passed through a 0.45-μm filter by pressure filtration to remove impurities. The filtered CQD was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 60 min.

Support loading type is done by impregnation and hydrothermal loading methods. For the Ru metal ratio, 0.05, 0.1, 0.2, 0.3 and 0.5 mmol values were used versus the specific CQD ratio. These methods include Ru impregnation loading on the CQD support obtained from caffeine without any treatment and Ru loading on the obtained CQD support material under hydrothermal conditions. The catalyst obtained by the precipitation method from Ru impregnation using CQD support was labelled Ru@CQD. The Ru catalyst obtained from CQD in a water by hydrothermal loading method was labelled Ru@CQD (water). Ru catalyst obtained from CQD in a methanol by hydrothermal loading method was labelled as Ru@CQD (methanol).

An assembly consisting of a water bath, magnetic stirrer, jacketed tube, gas burette and connecting hoses was used to catalyze the hydrolysis of NH3BH3 with CQD-supported Ru catalysts for the production of H2. In the experimental phase, an amount of catalysts and an amount of NaOH were first added to a bottle, and then the bottle was closed and placed in a water bath. NH3BH3 was added to the flask to initiate the reaction. The volume of H2 obtained was measured in a scaled burette by the water displacement method. In NH3BH3 hydrolysis, the NH3BH3 concentration, the NaOH concentration, the effects of solvents and the effects of temperature were investigated. The performance of the catalyst was evaluated by the mean hydrogen production rate (HGR) defined as mLmin−1gcat −1 and as TOF. Repeatability experiments of the catalyst were performed six times in succession. After each experiment, the same amount of NH3BH3 and NaOH was added to the reaction medium.

The spectrum of UV–Vis absorption of CQD and Ru@CQD samples from caffeine is given in Fig. 1 a. CQDs showed a UV–vis absorbance spectrum with a broad absorbance peak between 240 and 300 nm. The maximum peak at 270 nm in the UV–Vis absorption of CQD from caffeine was attributed to the II-II∗ transition of conjugated CC units [29]. Fig. 1b gives the PL absorption spectrum of CQD and Ru@CQD samples. The PL absorption spectrum of the aqueous solution of CQD led to the highest emission peak at 355 nm. Adding Ru to the CQD decreased the intensity of the fluorescent emission of the CQD. This result indicates that Ru ions weaken the fluorescence effect of CQDs. The probable reason for this result can be attributed to the quenching of the fluorescence of CQDs by complexing of CQDs and metal ions [30,31] (see Fig. 1b).

The morphology and microstructure of CQD (a, b), Ru@CQD (water) (c, d) and Ru@CQD (methanol) (e, f) nanocomposites were performed by TEM analysis (Fig. 2 ). Ru nanoparticles on the CQD surface showed a fairly homogeneous distribution without aggregation. The average particle size of CQD (caffeine) particles was 5.52 nm. However, the average particle sizes of Ru@CQD (water) and Ru@CQD (methanol) nanoparticles were about 13.52 and 1.81 nm. As can be understood, the particle size value of the Ru@CQD (water) sample is larger compared to the CQD (caffeine) sample, while the particle size value of the Ru@CQD (methanol) sample is quite small. Metal particle size is one of the main parameters that affect the catalytic activity and performance of supported catalysts. The particle size and distribution of Ru nanoparticles on the CQD support can be attributed to important electron-rich coordination sites, which can both coordinate the Ru ions in solution and effectively prevent their aggregation. At the same time, in the methanol environment on the CQD, a faster and more homogeneous reduction of Ru ions with NH3BH3 may occur. It has been stated that the overgrowth of Ru nanoparticles can be effectively prevented as a result of rapid reduction with their different electronic properties. The result affect the catalytic activity either by facilitating the decomposition of the reactants or by stabilizing the intermediate reaction types [32]. For example, strong adsorption of species such as H, OH and CO on metal particles has been reported in the literature when the particle size of carbon-supported Pt clusters is below 5 nm [33]. At the same time, it has been stated that smaller Pt particles can change the catalytic activity by increasing the charge transfer between Pt particles as a result of the interaction of the smaller Pt particles with the carbon support more easily compared to the larger particles [34].

According to another report, it was stated in a study on CQDs with an average particle size of 2.3 nm that it has good mono-dispersity. The result obtained for Ru@CQD(methanol) at 1.81 nm agrees with the values observed for CQDs in the range of 1.3–3.7 nm [35].

To understand the interaction between FT−IR characterization and Ru@CQD nanoclusters prepared by the hydrothermal method in water and methanol medium, surface groups were examined as shown in Fig. 3 . Although both FTIR spectra are similar to each other, there are some differences for the Ru@CQD sample prepared in methanol by the hydrothermal method. These differences are due to the vibration of the oxygenated groups, especially at the peaks of approximately 1200 cm−1 and 1700 cm−1. After RuCl3 was mixed with CQD by the hydrothermal method for 8 h, the Ru particles remained in the solution rather than clinging to the surface of the support. In the production of CQDs, a high-temperature hydrothermal method is used that allows the interaction between precursors and water molecules [36,37]. Better surface passivation and photoluminescent properties of CQDs can be achieved with high production yields [24]. At the same time, the abundance of oxygenated functional feedstock groups contributes to the formation of carbon nuclei and surface functionalization of CQDs [25].

The actual active Ru loading in the Ru@CQD (water) sample was found to be 41% by ICP-MS. However, the actual active Ru loading in the Ru@CQD (methanol) sample was found to be 23% by weight. The amount of Ru obtained for Ru@CQD (methanol) is much lower than for Ru@CQD (water).

For the homogeneous Ru@CQDs catalyst used in NH3BH3 hydrolysis, the effect of different Ru mole ratios against the constant CQD source with hydrothermal heating in a water medium and without hydrothermal treatment was investigated. The volumes of H2 obtained from the hydrolysis of NH3BH3 as a result of loading operations at 0.05, 0.1, 0.2, 0.3 and 0.5 mmol are given in Fig. 4 . HGR values obtained with CQD-supported 0.05, 0.1, 0.2, 0.3 and 0.5 mmol Ru ratios, respectively, were found to be 34129, 38252, 45856, 31410 and 28357 mLmin−1gcat −1. As can be understood, the best metal ratio was obtained with the use of 0.2 mmol Ru. In the metal loading type, besides hydrothermal loading, Ru loading by impregnation was also investigated. An HGR of 18000 mLmin−1gcat −1 was obtained with the catalyst obtained as a result of impregnation with 0.2 mmol Ru on CQD. It has been determined that the impregnation method performs as low as 2.5 times compared to hydrothermal loading. Support materials used in catalytic reactions can produce different catalytic performances due to their structures and active site-support interactions. It seems that the metal particle size on Ru@CQD (methanol) is much smaller and more active, thus contributing to good catalytic performance. This is because CQDs synthesized by hydrothermal can form complexes with Ru+3 and then settle into the interior of the support. In the absence of CQDs, however, the interaction between Ru+3 and support is too weak to retain Ru+3, resulting in Ru+3 loss during filtration and washing [31]. As explained from TEM analyses, Ru@CQD samples prepared in water and methanol have a nanoscale structure. Studies have shown that nanoscale metal can significantly increase efficiency in catalytic reactions [38,39]. It has been stated that the catalytic activity and selectivity are largely related to the particle size per surface metal atom [31]. At the same time, active sites of CQDs containing various functional groups such as amine and carboxyl groups can bind metal ions by electrostatic attraction and π-π interactions [40].

In most of the hydrothermal production methods of CQDs from raw materials, deionized water is chosen as the reaction medium because the carbon sources are water-soluble and well-dispersible. However, if there are oxygen-functional groups in the chemical structures of the raw materials, other options can also be evaluated [22]. In the hydrolytic dehydrogenation of NH3BH3 at 30 °C, the solvent effect was also investigated to improve the catalytic performance of CQD-supported catalysts by hydrothermal treatment. Water, methanol and ethanol were investigated as the solution medium and the obtained H2 production volumes are given in Fig. 5 . HGR values of 45856, 89205 and 69537 mLmin−1gcat −1 were obtained with Ru@CQD prepared in water, methanol and ethanol medium, respectively. As can be understood, different solvents have important effects on catalyst production. In addition, the performance of the catalytic reaction can be significantly improved by changing the activation energy of the reaction intermediate complex or adjacent metal active sites with solvent adsorption on the catalyst [41].

The effect of NH3BH3 concentration on the catalytic hydrolysis reaction of NH3BH3 with Ru@CQD prepared by hydrothermal method with CQD support in methanol and water medium was carried out at 30 °C. Fig. 6 a and b shows the volume of H2 produced from this catalytic hydrolysis at different concentrations of NH3BH3 with Ru@CQD catalysts prepared in water and methanol solvents. The HGR and TOF values obtained with Ru@CQD (water) and Ru@CQD (methanol) at 0.5, 1, 3, and 5 mmol NH3BH3 concentrations are given in Table 1 . As can be seen, there is an increase in the obtained HGR and TOF values depending on the increase in NH3BH3 concentration. Also, considerably higher values were obtained with Ru@CQD (methanol) compared to the Ru@CQD (water) catalyst. TOF values of 361 and 18806 min−1 were obtained with Ru@CQD (water) and Ru@CQD (methanol) at 5 mmol NH3BH3 concentration at 30 °C, respectively.

In an aqueous solution of NH3BH3, there is usually faster hydrolysis with the appropriate amount of NaOH [42]. Fig. 7 a and b shows the H2 volumes obtained with Ru@CQD (water) and Ru@CQD (methanol) at 5 mmol NH3BH3 concentration and different NaOH concentrations at 30 °C, respectively. 0%, 1%, 2.5%, 5% and 7.5% were chosen as NaOH concentrations. The HGR values obtained with Ru@CQD (water) from NH3BH3 hydrolysis containing 0%, 1%, 2.5%, 5% and 7.5% NaOH, respectively, were 36318, 62309, 71745, 78008 and 69034 mLmin−1gcat −1. However, HGR values of 89205, 190849, 248285, 222577, and 201407 mLmin−1gcat −1, respectively, were obtained with Ru@CQD (methanol) at the same NaOH concentrations. NaOH concentration has a significant effect on H2 production from NH3BH3 hydrolysis with both catalysts. As can be understood, for H2 production from Ru@CQD(water) NH3BH3 hydrolysis, the highest HGR was obtained with 5% NaOH, while the highest HGR with Ru@CQD(methanol) was obtained with 2.5% NaOH. Promoting the cleavage of the O–H bond in the H2O molecule and activation of the NH3BH3 molecule can be shown as the reason for the positive effect of NaOH. The increase in solution viscosity can be demonstrated by causing the HGR values to decrease from the maximum at higher NaOH concentrations [43].

Fig. 8 a and b shows the volumes of H2 obtained at different temperatures at a concentration of 5 mmol NH3BH3 with Ru@CQD (water) and Ru@CQD (methanol), respectively. 30, 40, 50, and 60 °C were selected as temperature values. The HGR values obtained with Ru@CQD(water) from NH3BH3 hydrolysis at 30, 40, 50, and 60 °C temperatures, respectively, were 113514, 211083, 348888 ve 503570 mLmin−1gcat −1. However, HGR values of 258854, 419488, 574611, and 817848 mLmin−1gcat −1, respectively, were obtained with Ru@CQD (methanol) at the same temperature values. As can be understood, there is a significant effect of temperature increase on H2 production from NH3BH3 hydrolysis with both catalysts. Fig. 9 a and b shows the Arrhenius plots (ln k vs 1/T) obtained with Ru@CQD (water) and Ru@CQD (methanol) at different temperatures, respectively. The Ea values obtained with Ru@CQD (water) and Ru@CQD (methanol) were found to be 33.32 kJ mol−1 and 27.80 kJ mol−1, respectively. The obtained TOF and Ea values were compared with different catalyst systems used for NH3BH3 in the literature (Table 2 ) [2,44–55]. As can be seen, the results obtained with CQD-supported Ru catalysts in different solvent environments by the hydrothermal method in this study can be considered very well.

Fig. 10 a and b shows the volumes of H2 obtained from six consecutive repeatability experiments of Ru@CQD(water) and Ru@CQD(methanol) catalysts to H2 production from NH3BH3 hydrolysis, respectively, and the percentage of H2 conversion from the hydrolysis reaction in each experiment. With both catalysts, there is 100% H2 conversion in all cycles. However, after the first use of catalysts in repeatability experiments, there are gradual decreases in catalytic activity in each experiment. For example, the reaction of H2 production from hydrolysis of NH3BH3 with Ru@CQD (water) finished in about 1.4 min in the first cycle and about 1.8 min in the sixth cycle. Likewise, the reaction of H2 production from the hydrolysis of NH3BH3 with Ru@CQD (methanol) finished in about 2 min in the first cycle and in about 4 min in the sixth cycle. One of the major disadvantages of homogeneous catalysis reactions is the difficulty of removing the catalyst from the environment. Repeated use of the catalyst due to the difficulty experienced in removal results in poor performance catalytic processes. However, when the data obtained for both catalysts are examined, there is a very high performance in repeated use within the scope of this study. It was observed that the catalysts formed agglomeration (agglomeration) and precipitated after the 4th use during the 30-min waiting period after repeated use.

TEM analyses indicated that the particle sizes of CQD, Ru@CQD (water) and Ru@CQD (methanol) are quite small and their morphology contains a large number of useable active sites without any aggregation. In this study, the catalyst with a nanoparticle size of 1.81 nm showed optimum catalytic activity for Ru@CQD (methanol). It contributes to a good catalytic performance by contributing to the mass transfer of the reactant and the product as a result of effectively preventing the aggregation of Ru particles with CQD. In addition, in this study, both the smaller Ru@CQD particles and the catalyst obtained by using methanol instead of water by the hydrothermal method have a significant contribution to the production of H2 from NH3BH3.

From the TEM analyses, the average Ru@CQD grain size was larger when water was used as the solvent compared to methanol. The use of water as the solvent results in the lowest catalytic activity, while the use of methanol as the solvent is much more effective in the catalytic activity of the Ru@CQD catalyst. For Cu-based catalysts, it has been stated that Cu particles accumulate easily at high temperatures in the water environment, leading to a decrease in active surface area and catalytic activity [56,57]. It has been explained that as a result of the higher surface tension of water compared to ethanol and methanol, the collection of Cu nanoparticles becomes easier and the active sites decrease as well as grain growth. However, it was reported that smaller Cu particle sizes and higher catalytic activity were achieved by preventing the aggregation of Cu nanoparticles in ethanol and methanol media with lower surface tensions [58]. The same annotation approach is used in the Ru particles used in this study. Also, polar water molecules tend to readily form hydrogen bonds with hydroxyl groups on the Ru@CQD surface. Thus, the distribution of Ru on the CQD is prevented, resulting in the formation of larger particles. Similar explanations exist in the literature [59]. Solvent viscosity also affects the coordination of solvent molecules with metals. There are nucleation and particle growth stages in the precipitation of metal nanoparticles in which the diffusion process is effective [60]. With the increase in solvent viscosity, a suitable environment for agglomeration will be created due to the decrease in the diffusion rates of the solution species. The viscosity values for water and methanol solvents were expressed as 1.793 and 0.793 mN s/m2, respectively [61,62]. Therefore, there will be increased agglomeration in the aquatic environment with a higher viscosity compared to methanol.

The initiation of hydrolysis of NH3BH3 is associated with the formation of an activated type of complex between NH3BH3 and the metal particle surface. FTIR results showed that especially for Ru@CQD(methanol), oxygen-containing groups were high. These results show that the high catalytic activity obtained for Ru@CQD is important not only with particle size but also the presence of oxidized species to catalyze NH3BH3 hydrolysis efficiently. There are similar approaches in the literature [63,64]. The metal-catalyzed hydrolysis of NH3BH3 takes place in the following steps [65]. After, ammonia hydroxide is released as a result of the reaction between H2O and NH3 ions. BH3 − adsorbed on Ru@CQD metal atoms transfer an H− to an adjacent free Ru metal atom. This H− metal leaves the site and reacts with H2O to form OH- and H2. Next, OH− with boron in BH2 − forms BH2(OH)-. After, H− from BH2(OH)- is transferred to an adjacent free metal atom to form BH(OH)-. This reaction is repeated until B(OH)3 - and H2 are released. It has been reported that the oxidized species in the catalyst act as Lewis acid sites for OH− absorption due to their strong affinity for the oxygen of the OH− group [66]. Thus, polarizing OH- groups will make them more prone to react with boron species attached to reduced metal sites. This reaction intermediate produces boric acid, with the formation of H2 by the attack of an H2O molecule and the cleavage of the BN bond and the hydrolysis of the resulting BH3 intermediate [65]. Therefore, the ability of CQD-supported Ru particles to form active complexes with NH3BH3 is important for its catalytic performance. At this point, it can be assumed that with a smaller Ru particle size, the ability to form an activated complex with Ru–NH3BH3 formed in the first step of the reaction may be higher. The production scheme of Ru@CQD (methanol or water) catalyst and H2 production from NH3BH3 hydrolysis are shown in Fig. 11 .

In summary, Ru nanoparticles on CQD support obtained from the caffeine in the methanol or water solvents by a hydrothermal process were used for the first time to produce effective H2 from NH3BH3 hydrolysis. The chemical compositions and morphology structures of this caffeine carbon dot-supported Ru catalysts were carefully characterized by UV, PL, TEM, FTIR, and ICP/OES analyses. Ru nanoparticles with a particle size of 1.81, which were quite well dispersed on the CQD support obtained by the hydrothermal method, especially in the methanol solvent, were determined from TEM analysis. Although FTIR spectra are similar to each other for the catalysts, there are some differences due to the vibration of the oxygenated groups for the Ru@CQD sample prepared in methanol by the hydrothermal method at the peaks of approximately 1200 cm−1 and 1700 cm−1. As a result, the catalytic activity of NH3BH3 hydrolysis on Ru@CQD(water) and Ru@CQD(methanol) was obtained at 30 °C with high TOF values of 361 and 1895 min−1, respectively. In particular, the TOF obtained for Ru@CQD (methanol) outperformed most of the reported Ru-based catalysts. In this study, efficient and environmentally friendly CQDs obtained from caffeine by hydrothermal method for Ru particles showed a significant catalytic performance for NH3BH3 hydrolysis with an electron-rich and strong hydrophilic region.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The Scientific Research Projects Coordinatorship of Siirt University has supported a part of this study with the project numbered 2022-SİÜFEB-010.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Promoting effect of transition metal-doped Co-B alloy catalysts for hydrogen production by hydrolysis of alkaline NaBH4 solution
J Catal

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Patel N, Fernandes R, Miotello A. Promoting effect of transition metal-doped Co-B alloy catalysts for hydrogen production by hydrolysis of alkaline NaBH4 solution. J Catal 2010;271:315-324. doi:10.1016/j.jcat.2010.02.014.
